content:
I am currently trying to get vscode working nicely with the rust-binder-rfc branch. The problem I am facing is that rust-analyzer seems to not understand the "Receiver" and similar unstable traits.

image.png

This is one of the resulting errors. Does anyone have any idea how this could be fixed, or is this just expected behavior? It really makes following the code quite hard, as "goto definition" and similar features also regularly break...
did you run make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer?
yes, the basic functionality is working normally, it's just that certain code triggers errors that are not actually there when building the kernel. It's purely a rust-analyzer issue I guess.
I also tried the specific version of rust-analyzer shipped with the rust release that this kernel uses
which goto definition doesn't work for you?

image.png

This would be an example, for some reason it seems that rust-analyzer does not recognize that this can be dereferenced to access get_thread and thus pressing F12 on it also does not work.
I might try to port the binder rust code to the rust-next branch and see if it solves the issues

Yeah, this also doesn't work for me, I'm using vim, so it's probably related to rust-analyzer. It seems the binder rust files are not even parsed by scripts/generate_rust_analyzer.py:

❯ make LLVM=1 rust-analyzer
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/samples/rust/rust_minimal.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Adding rust_minimal
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/samples/rust/rust_print.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Adding rust_print
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/samples/rust/hostprogs/a.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/samples/rust/hostprogs/b.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,860] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/samples/rust/hostprogs/single.rs

[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/thread.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/context.rs

[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/process.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/allocation.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/error.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/node.rs
[2024-07-03 12:00:07,862] [INFO] Checking /home/amiculas/work/linux/drivers/android/defs.rs

I tried adding them but there's no improvement, the goto definition still doesn't work for get_thread and others
I also tried using a newer state of the linux kernel (android15-6.6 branch in the android kernel repo) and now it does not even recognize the binder rust files as being in the project at all...
Did you enable the binder in the config?
no, good point
I did enable it now and I get the same result
I am out of ideas of what could be the problem here, but I cannot imagine that this is the state that everyone else just accepted, it's pretty hard to develop like this...
@Alice Ryhl works on binder, maybe she has some ideas about this
I actually asked her already :D
Sorry, I don't know if go-to-definition works in my setup. I have not tried it.
The root problem seems to be rust-analyzer not recognizing the Receiver trait
This is the problem I am trying to solve, I would guess this error was not present on your end
Try looking in the rust analyzer repository for issues about that?

But just to be sure, when developing the android rust binder code you had proper ide support and no errors shown in your neovim setup? Just trying to figure out how vscode seems to be breaking rust-analyzer more than neovim
Well, tab completion works, at least. I don't really use any of the other IDE features.
oh ok then I guess this issue wouldn't even apply to you when present in your setup.
I have the same issues in my PuzzleFS driver, some goto definitions work, but most of them don't

Ok actually I just found the problem.

The root cause of all problems is that generate_rust_analyzer.py includes the "core" crate in the rust-project.json file.
When I remove the logic to add this crate to the file, everything works wonderfully.

It seems that rust-analyzer breaks apart when this file explicitly include the core crate. Not sure if this is an actual bug or if it is simply not expected to include core in rust-project.json
I think there are actually more problems with generate_rust_bindings.py, as it does not correctly include the "uapi" crate

Rob said:

Ok actually I just found the problem.

The root cause of all problems is that generate_rust_analyzer.py includes the "core" crate in the rust-project.json file.
When I remove the logic to add this crate to the file, everything works wonderfully.

It seems that rust-analyzer breaks apart when this file explicitly include the core crate. Not sure if this is an actual bug or if it is simply not expected to include core in rust-project.json

Nice find, it works for me too.

Thanks :)

I am just not sure if I am really the first one to notice this problem or if this problem did not trigger in rust-analyzer for the people who implemented this logic...
I did notice it, then used grep and moved on :)
guess I will move on as well then
I think rust-analyzer is very useful, so if we could figure out how to fix it, it would be a great addition
especially compared to C, where code navigation is a real pain
Well in that case I can send a patch somewhere that fixes all of this :) just not sure where

Ariel Miculas schrieb:

especially compared to C, where code navigation is a real pain

That's very true, once I got the rust-analyzer navigation working it is not comparable to C
https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing
Thx, I will work on this asap
Did I understand correctly that the best way to get feedback is to open a PR to the rust-next branch before submitting to the mailing list?
I don't think that's necessary. You can just submit to the mailing list directly.
You will receive feedback on your submission as email replies.

I also get this error

Initializing Rust completer: warning - Failed spawning proc-macro server for workspace /home/amiculas/work/linux/rust-project.json: no sysroot

which goes away when I add sysroot to the rust-project.json

yes there seems to be a difference between rust-analyzer versions.
when using the one shoipped with 1.78.0 "sysroot_src" works
the later versions complain about missing sysroot
but i will add a fix that just sets both keys in the rust-project.json file
awesome!
Ok I think this problem is deeper than I though, different rust-analyzer versions require different options, but the version used by the current kernel has no good solution available it seems

isn't rust-analyzer's version pinned to the rustc version?
I see

$ rustup show
active toolchain
----------------
1.78.0-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu (directory override for '/home/amiculas/work/linux')
rustc 1.78.0 (9b00956e5 2024-04-29)
$ rust-analyzer --version
rust-analyzer 1.78.0 (9b00956 2024-04-29)
$ rustc --version
rustc 1.78.0 (9b00956e5 2024-04-29)
yes it is, but you can just as well run any other rust-analyzer executable, which is what vscode does by default
and I cannot find a good solution for the one that is pinned by the kernel repo
hmm, my vim setup also uses another version of rust-analyzer; I don't know if there's a good solution, other than making sure it works with the rust-analyzer version that matches rustc's version
well as there is no good solution for this version, I would propose to make it work with more recent versions
which version are you talking about?
maybe it would be worth mentioning it here: https://docs.kernel.org/rust/quick-start.html#rust-analyzer
yes I guess I will try to find a good setup with a specific rust-analyzer version and make changes to the generator accordingly. will take some time though
So if you install it via rustup component add rust-analyzer, it doesn't work?
at least not very good, I will investigate further when I have time
there must be some solution for the rust-analyzer version of 1.78.0
well the toolchain will keep updating, so maybe it's not worth the effort to deal with the old versions
would think so as well

We are starting to support several toolchain versions, including Rust 1.78.0, 1.79.0, beta and nightly soon (https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240701183625.665574-1-ojeda@kernel.org/), so ideally we would make it work as good as it can work for each version.
that's great, didn't realize we're at this step already
The question would be if we have to rely on the rust-analyzer version shipped with these respective toolchains or just have the users install some minimum version that's not tied to the kernel
What do you mean by "tied to the kernel"? Do you mean the one shipped by rustup?
i.e. we don't tie any version (for rust-analyzer)

I mean do we rely on the user installing the rust-analyzer via rustup (meaning pinned to the respective toolchain version used by the kernel) or can we say that the user should install version XXX of rust-analyzer via any means? this would allow requiring a minimum rust-analyzer version higher than the one shipped with 1.78.0
which would make fixing the problems somewhat easier
I think the users will want to use the rust-analyzer version that's packaged with the respective toolchain versions

We could have a different minimum if it is needed, but I think the priorities are:

  • Make it work as best as it can be with the versions shipped by major Linux distributions (which I would guess are similar to the version of rustc they ship, but perhaps they update rust-analyzer independently? i.e. they may not take the one that was shipped by rustup for a given rustc).
  • Make it work with rustup's rust-analyzer (for the Rust versions the kernel supports)
  • Make it work with other rust-analyzer versions.

(the first two bullet points imply newer/upcoming versions too)

I mean do we rely on the user installing the rust-analyzer via rustup (meaning pinned to the respective toolchain version used by the kernel)

Regarding users: they can install the toolchains however they want, that is, they may install things via their Linux distribution, or via rustup, or manually, or they may build the toolchain.

To be clear: very soon we will not be pinning a Rust version in the kernel, i.e. we will have only a minimum (1.78.0 being the first minimum).
ok in that case I will have to try to find a better solution for the 1.78.0 rust-analyzer release, but I am not sure there is one
Thanks a lot @Rob for taking a look at this, by the way.
no problem, I would love to get into kernel rust dev :)
If you think supporting 1.78.0 is hard (i.e. the workaround is not worth the pain), we could always choose to keep it partially supported, i.e. as it is now.
ok I will keep that in mind, but will try to find an adequate solution first

Rust-analyzer is supposed to support the last couple of rustc releases. Most people use the version bundled with the vscode extension which gets the latest rust-analyzer changes every week (or every day if you use the nightly version of the vscode extension). This version is supposed to work with both the current nightly as well as the current stable. By using the version shipped with rustup, you are missing out on 6-12 weeks worth of fixes (and potentially regressions)
I agree, I am also using the vscode extension, meaning I have a version that would allow for an easy fix
But maybe I can find a solution for 1.78.0

Rob said:

The question would be if we have to rely on the rust-analyzer version shipped with these respective toolchains or just have the users install some minimum version that's not tied to the kernel

I think the rust analyzer shipped with rustup is not the recommended way to get it. I remember reading that somewhere. You should get it from rust analyzer git instead. But maybe that changed?

I am trying to get into linux development with rust and just encountered this issue while using VS code.
I think we should set the sysroot instead of sysroot_src in the rust-project.json. sysroot has been supported as an option for the past 2 years since https://github.com/rust-lang/rust-analyzer/pull/12858/files#diff-9b5063793cdc53c613ee6a1e824bb6c176cb4aca1b27c6d7cd9c22a568e79eeeR21
It should work well for any version of rust released in the past two years.
If you all agree that this is the right way to go I would like to contribute this as my first pr.

You are right that this option is supported for long enough, the problem I found is that the 1.73.0 rust-analyzer still infers the sysroot_src fron the sysroot, breaking the core crate again. But I would also say that setting the sysroot instead of sysroot_src is better than nothing

I guess I'm the first one to report this but the patch rust: Support latest version of \rust-analyzer\` broke the rust-analyzer target for me. Relevant build logs with V=12`:

linux> sh ../scripts/rust_is_available.sh
linux> ../scripts/generate_rust_analyzer.py \
linux>  --cfgs='core=--cfg no_fp_fmt_parse' --cfgs='alloc=--cfg no_global_oom_handling --cfg no_rc --cfg no_sync' \
linux>  /build/linux-6.11 /build/linux-6.11/build \
linux>  /nix/store/p024jn1sd4pn33lg0kaqxh7j2sp1iw7x-rustc-1.80.1 /nix/store/5dh2vfghjizfwgci0z00hbrpx5vwv6zc-rust-lib-src  > \
linux>  ./rust-project.json
linux> make -f ../scripts/Makefile.build obj=rust
linux> Traceback (most recent call last):
linux>   File "/build/linux-6.11/build/../scripts/generate_rust_analyzer.py", line 171, in <module>
linux>     main()
linux>   File "/build/linux-6.11/build/../scripts/generate_rust_analyzer.py", line 161, in main
linux>     assert args.sysroot in args.sysroot_src.parents
linux>            ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
linux> AssertionError
linux> make[2]: *** [../rust/Makefile:350: rust-analyzer] Error 1
linux> make[1]: *** [/build/linux-6.11/Makefile:1968: rust-analyzer] Error 2
linux> make[1]: *** Waiting for unfinished jobs....

Would "check if the sysroot path and sysroot_src path has /nix/store in it and if yes, do ignore the assertion" be a patch that upstream would accept?
[incomplete message sent by error, redacted]

Also, another question: I reverted said patch to get a working rust-project.json and got this:

$ grep 'display_name\|root_module' rust-project.json
            "display_name": "core",

This is more of a TODO but I think /build/linux-<ver> is the wrong path. It should be /nix/store/<sha>-linux-<ver>-dev/lib/modules/6.11.0/source since that's where the -devel equivalent package files (once built) are stored in NixOS. Someone correct me if I'm wrong.
I am also on nix and I don't have this problem with my setup, what are you using to install rust?

I have applied this patch to nixpkgs for now to build the rust-analyzer target:

diff --git a/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix b/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
index 5544409a74d6..64c8dee911ba 100644
--- a/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
+++ b/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
@@ -113,6 +113,7 @@ let

       isModular = config.isYes "MODULES";
       withRust = config.isYes "RUST";
+      rustAnalyzerOutput = if withRust then ",rust-project.json" else ""; # copy the 'rust-project.json' if the Kernel is built with Rust support enabled

       buildDTBs = kernelConf.DTB or false;

@@ -248,8 +249,9 @@ let
         "KBUILD_BUILD_VERSION=1-NixOS"
         kernelConf.target
         "vmlinux"  # for "perf" and things like that
-      ] ++ optional isModular "modules"
+      ] ++ optionals isModular ["modules"]
         ++ optionals buildDTBs ["dtbs" "DTC_FLAGS=-@"]
+        ++ optionals withRust  ["rust-analyzer"]
       ++ extraMakeFlags;

       installFlags = [
@@ -341,7 +343,7 @@ let

         cd $dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/source

-        cp $buildRoot/{.config,Module.symvers} $dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build
+        cp $buildRoot/{.config,Module.symvers${rustAnalyzerOutput}} $dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build
         make modules_prepare $makeFlags "''${makeFlagsArray[@]}" O=$dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build

         # For reproducibility, removes accidental leftovers from a `cc1` call

But, I believe the following patch should work better but that would come after the entire build and I will get to it after generating the rust-project.json.

diff --git a/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix b/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
index 5544409a74d6..913e235cd111 100644
--- a/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
+++ b/pkgs/os-specific/linux/kernel/manual-config.nix
@@ -113,6 +113,7 @@ let

       isModular = config.isYes "MODULES";
       withRust = config.isYes "RUST";
+      rustAnalyzerTarget = if withRust then "rust-analyzer" else ""; # builds the `rust-analyzer` target if the Kernel is built with Rust support

       buildDTBs = kernelConf.DTB or false;

@@ -248,7 +249,7 @@ let
         "KBUILD_BUILD_VERSION=1-NixOS"
         kernelConf.target
         "vmlinux"  # for "perf" and things like that
-      ] ++ optional isModular "modules"
+      ] ++ optionals isModular ["modules"]
         ++ optionals buildDTBs ["dtbs" "DTC_FLAGS=-@"]
       ++ extraMakeFlags;

@@ -342,7 +343,7 @@ let
         cd $dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/source

         cp $buildRoot/{.config,Module.symvers} $dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build
-        make modules_prepare $makeFlags "''${makeFlagsArray[@]}" O=$dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build
+        make modules_prepare ${rustAnalyzerTarget} $makeFlags "''${makeFlagsArray[@]}" O=$dev/lib/modules/${modDirVersion}/build

         # For reproducibility, removes accidental leftovers from a `cc1` call
         # from a `try-run` call from the Makefile

@Benno Lossin I'm performing a build with allmodconfig rust.config but I don't think that it's relevant. What I think is relevant is the kernel version being built. I'm building the 6.11 (non -rc) kernel.
ah I see, you're building the kernel itself and not developing on it
Yes
then I cannot be of much help to you, as I have no expertise in that area. Maybe @Fiona Behrens can help you more
why do you need to build rust analyzer though?
No real reason other than "Maybe I should?" :D
If you're building the kernel for your system and not going to do development, then rust-analyzer is rather useless, right? (or am I forgetting some other usage?)

I do realize that it's quite useless. Just wanted it for a niche use-case where I change the working machine based on what's closest to me and having the json file on every one of those dev machines would be nice. :)
I see, I don't think there is anything against changing how we generate that file, but I think it would be best if the check just worked
What's more surprising to me is how rust is even being enabled with the compiler being GCC.
^ in nixpkgs
I thought using CLang was a requirement.

clang is not a requirement. There is a large group user which use gcc+rust built kernels with a non-trivial rust driver: Fedora Asahi Remix uses gcc to build the C kernel code like Fedora. Fedora itself will turn rust on their kernel soon now that it is reasonable to expect that the distro packaged rust can used to build the kernel.
Huh, I assumed that setting LLVM was a requirement as the docs mention it in quite a few examples.

Pratham Patel said:

I do realize that it's quite useless. Just wanted it for a niche use-case where I change the working machine based on what's closest to me and having the json file on every one of those dev machines would be nice. :)

not really understand the usecase yet. You either build the kernel for you system, which is then immutable where the rust-project does not make any sense as everything is immutable, or you are working on the kernel where the whole tree including rust-project should be mutable, as it's dynamic according to the changes you do

@Miguel Ojeda I just stumbled upon https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/commit/fe992163575b187405899c5abaad8ef6fb828ff6 which mentions this thread - I think the commit message is wrong:

Sets the sysroot field in rust-project.json which is now needed in newer versions of rust-analyzer instead of the sysroot_src field.

In fact this version of the patch sets both sysroot and sysroot_src. Was that intentional to avoid changing the author's wording?
Oops, my mistake - it just kept both flags to the script, but indeed no longer sets sysroot_src.
Ah, OK :) I was about to test because I couldn't see it set from a quick glance.
Sorry for the false alarm!
No worries at all! Thanks for reporting it nevertheless.
Has anyone else noticed that it seems like rust-analyzer doesn't have any clear understanding of what crates are present in the rust/macros/ folder?
what do you mean with crates in that folder?

so - I need to double check if it's just the branch I'm on since I think I have some parts of proc_macros2 pulled in (one of the branches rvkms depends on is Gerry's more recent #[vtable] macro with #[unique] support). But basically whenever I open up any of the source files in that folder rust-analyzer doesn't seem to be able to provide me any kind of documentation or information on any items from the proc_macro crate

image.png
normally everything like TokenStream would have different highlighting, and asking rust-analyzer for documentation on those items doesn't bring anything up
first guess would be that proc_macro is missing in the rust-analyzer.json or is not correctly declared for macros. but can't check right now, not at my kernel dev setup right now
just checked with just reading the source. seems like as I suspected, it's missing in the python script, can try to fix it tomorrow
ooo that would be super helpful, thank you! happy to provide reviews if it helps
completion inside proc_macro is unecessary, right? that would be much harder because we don't have std properly. my lsp has not at least stuff like TokenStream

Ah oh, macros also depends on std, and alloc (though std). guess we can just add it, but without all the dependencies. autocompletion might be not fully working, but I think it still we be a massive imrpovement to also have std(/alloc)
I mean it would be prett helpful if we could get completion for proc_macro using the built-in proc_macro crate, but whatever you're able to do helps
Not sure I understand what you mean, I now just showed rust analyzer where the source for proc macro is

Hi, I am just getting started with "rust for linux" and wanted to set up an environment for out of tree modules. I am on NixOS so getting the rust-analyzer target to work was a bit messy (due to this issue), but I managed to get there with a hacky solution.

Now, although I am getting auto-completion support and docs, I noticed that I wasn't getting any diagnostics from the LSP (wrong types, unbound names, etc). After looking at the rust-analyzer docs once more, I saw that it required me to provide it with a custom "check" command to get all of those features back when using rust-project.json. Perhaps this is related to #General > Alternative to `cargo check`, but I don't see any mention of this in the "rust for linux" wiki. Is there any recommended solution for this?

I am on .vscode for Rust, and added this to my settings.json:

  "rust-analyzer.check.overrideCommand": [
    "bash",
    "-c",
    "make -s \"KRUSTFLAGS+=--error-format=json\" 2>&1 | grep -v \"^make\""
  ]

Which seems to somewhat fix it, although when there are no issues in the code, rust-analyzer complains because the above returns nothing. Also, that command will sometimes output lines that are neither json nor start with make, so perhaps that could be a problem as well...

Also, that command will sometimes output lines that are neither json nor start with make, so perhaps that could be a problem as well...

Rust-analyzer filters out all lines that don't start with { I believe, so that shouldn't be an issue.
